title: Entropy transport through a superfluid quantum point contact: A Keldysh field-theory approach
authors: Davide Bertolusso, C. J. Bolech, Thierry Giamarchi
abstract: We study the matter and entropy transport between two ultra-cold neutral Fermi-gas reservoirs linked by a quantum point contact under a chemical-potential gradient. We describe the two leads with a BCS mean-field model and derive the current-bias characteristics for both particle and entropy transport. We compute the out of equilibrium steady-state currents by using the Keldysh formalism. In accordance with previous works in the literature, we confirm the well-known behavior for the particle current and extend the computation to the entropy current in the BCS regime. The entropy current shows an oscillatory behavior at low voltage in the ballistic junction limit. We analyze the results for a wide range of values of the junction's transparency. We also compare our findings with experimental results in cold atomic gases in the unitary regime.
summary: This paper uses Keldysh field theory to study how particles and entropy flow through a superfluid quantum point contact. It demonstrates that the non-monotonic, oscillatory behavior of the entropy current is a direct consequence of Multiple Andreev Reflection processes. The work highlights the necessity of including particle-hole asymmetry to accurately model heat and entropy transport.
main_problem: The study investigates the mechanisms of particle and entropy transport through a superfluid quantum point contact, specifically focusing on the origin of oscillatory behavior in the entropy current.
main_result: The authors find that entropy current oscillations are driven by the activation of successive Multiple Andreev Reflection (MAR) processes rather than the divergence of the local density of states, and that quadratic dispersion is essential to capture heat transport.
method: A Keldysh field-theory approach is used to compute out-of-equilibrium steady-state currents, utilizing a Nambu-spinor representation and frequency-space analysis.
model_or_system: The system consists of two ultra-cold neutral Fermi-gas reservoirs in a superfluid state connected by a quantum point contact, modeled using BCS mean-field theory and a tunneling Hamiltonian with quadratic dispersion.
key_observables: Particle current (JN), heat current (JH), and entropy current (JS).
important_parameters: Chemical potential difference (delta-mu), temperature difference (delta-T), superconducting gap (delta), and junction transparency (T).
main_assumptions: The reservoirs are assumed to be in separate local equilibria, and the study focuses on the DC component of the currents, averaging out AC components.
figures_summary: Schematics of the junction and Andreev processes (Fig 1, 2, 3, 10); comparison of LDoS for linear vs. quadratic dispersion (Fig 5); current behavior in different junction types (Fig 6); and the persistence of oscillations in a truncated model (Fig 12).
paper_structure: The paper introduces the transport problem, describes the Keldysh formalism and Hamiltonian, analyzes the role of dispersion and Andreev reflections, presents results for SS and SN junctions, and compares findings with experimental cold-atom data.
why_interesting: It provides deep insight into non-equilibrium thermodynamics and transport in many-body systems, specifically how microscopic pairing mechanisms (MAR) manifest as macroscopic oscillatory transport properties in superfluid/superconducting junctions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Illustration of a two-reservoir system coupled at a single point x = 0, filled with an s-wave superfluid, where the reservoirs may have distinct temperatures and chemical potentials. This graphical representation shows the particle, JN, and entropy, JS, currents flowing along the x-direction.

FIG. 2. Depiction of a Andreev composite tunneling process at zero temperature (Tc = Td = 0) by involving two virtual Cooper pairs. Here, the chemical potential drop enters as a shift rather than as an explicit time dependence in the tunnel- ing as in Eq.(1), and it is numerically set at ∆µ = 1.2∆. The light -green process represents a particle from the left reservoir that tunnels elastically to the right while entangled with two virtual Cooper pairs, one on each lead (or via two successive Andreev reflections). The light-blue is the dual hole process starting from the right reservoir. Black (white) circles repre- sent spin-up particles (spin-down holes) and on the vertical axis we give the...

FIG. 4. Graphical representation of the non-equilibrium closed-path Keldysh integral. On the upper branch is where the forward field lives; instead, the backward field lives on the lower branch of the path.

FIG. 3. Depiction of a single Andreev reflection process at zero temperature (Tc = Td = 0). In this representation, the chemical potential enters as a shift rather than an explicit time dependence in the tunneling term as in Eq.(1), and it is numerically set at ∆µ = 1.2∆, as in Fig. 2. The light-green process represents a particle from the left reservoir that is being reflected as a hole to the same reservoir via one virtual Cooper pair on the right reservoir. The light-blue instead is the dual hole representation starting from the right reservoir. Black (white) circles represent spin-up particles (spin-down holes).
